source_case: Voss v. Black & Decker Mfg. Co., 59 N.Y.2d 102 (1983)
doctrinal_framework: Wade seven-factor risk-utility (as adopted by N.Y. Ct. App. in Voss)
status_note: Reclassified 2026-04-11 after round 2 face-validity review (REVIEW_v0.2.md finding F8). The 0.14-0.18 weight distribution across six factors is a constructor artifact, not a Voss artifact — real Wade-factor opinions do not distribute factors within a 0.04 band. This instance is preserved as a structural construction exercise that validated the JSON schema and taxonomy v1.2, but it is NOT a draft primary-dataset instance and its weight_sum_check should not be trusted. A rebuilt version with honest weight elicitation (expected skew toward user_awareness + product_utility + burden_of_precaution, consistent with the Wade-case defense theory) must be drafted after primary-text retrieval from CourtListener bulk data. That rebuild will also add a separate cluster for Wade factor 5 (user's ability to avoid danger), which the constructor incorrectly treated as fused with user_awareness.

source_verification.secondary_source_basis: Justia Supreme Court case digest + academic secondary sources cited in CASE_CANDIDATES.md round 1 retrieval
pending_for_conference_submission: Re-retrieve 59 N.Y.2d 102 from CourtListener bulk-data dump
Confirm factor enumeration matches opinion text verbatim
Elicit in-case weights from opinion rationale per SPEC.md §5.1 — do NOT reuse the fabricated v0.1 weights
Add separate Wade 5 cluster (user's ability to avoid danger) distinct from Wade 6 (user awareness)

case_background: Plaintiff Voss suffered serious hand injury while operating a Black & Decker table saw that was not equipped with a blade guard. He sued on design-defect grounds, arguing the saw should have shipped with a guard. The New York Court of Appeals adopted the Wade-factor risk-utility test and, weighing the seven factors, affirmed the defense verdict — the jury's conclusion that the design was not defectively unreasonable was supported by the factor balance.

factors.text: The probability that an unguarded table-saw blade would injure a user of ordinary skill operating the saw for its intended purpose.
factors.weight_justification: Court acknowledged non-trivial probability of kickback and blade contact but noted industry experience and user training reduced realized incidents
factors.counterfactual_note: If probability were dispositively high, the design would be defective regardless of other factors.

factors.text: The seriousness of injury from contact with an unguarded table-saw blade — typically severe (hand or finger amputation, lacerations, tendon damage).
factors.weight_justification: Severe when realized; court did not dispute the magnitude of potential injury
factors.counterfactual_note: If the realized harm were minor (e.g., minor cut), the risk-utility balance would tip the other way.

factors.text: Availability of a safer alternative design: blade guards were commercially feasible and used by some competitors in 1983, but interfered with certain cuts.
factors.weight_justification: Court acknowledged guards existed but were not universally adopted and impaired some legitimate uses
factors.counterfactual_note: If no alternative design existed, the risk-utility factor would strongly favor defendant.

factors.wade_factor_origin: Wade 4 (ability to eliminate unsafe character without impairing utility or unreasonable cost)
factors.text: Cost of adding a blade guard to the design and the utility impairment the guard would cause for cuts the guard would block.
factors.weight_justification: Court treated utility impairment as substantial — guards blocked certain professional cuts — alongside modest monetary cost
factors.counterfactual_note: If a guard could be added at zero utility cost, this factor would collapse toward liability.

factors.text: User's anticipated awareness that a table-saw blade is dangerous and requires careful operation — common knowledge for users of power tools.
factors.weight_justification: Court placed significant weight on the obviousness of the hazard; table-saw blades are openly recognized as dangerous
factors.counterfactual_note: If the hazard were non-obvious (e.g., hidden mechanism), this factor would invert.

factors.text: Usefulness and desirability of table saws for legitimate woodworking, including professional uses that a fixed blade guard would prevent.
factors.weight_justification: Court recognized the saw's substantial utility to skilled users and the fact that some uses require an unguarded blade
factors.counterfactual_note: If the product's utility could be preserved with a guard, this factor would be near zero.

weight_sum_check.note: Weights are constructor estimates, not elicited from the opinion text. Round 2 review flagged that the 0.04-band distribution is a tell: real Wade opinions skew toward the case's defense theory (likely ~0.25 user_awareness / 0.22 product_utility / 0.20 burden / 0.15 alt_precautions / 0.10 probability / 0.08 gravity for a Voss-style defense verdict). DO NOT report this instance's feature-3 status until weights are re-elicited from primary text.

question: Under the Wade seven-factor risk-utility test, was the Black & Decker table saw design defective because it lacked a blade guard?

ground_truth.rationale: Weighing all seven Wade factors, the product's utility combined with user awareness of the obvious hazard, the burden/utility-impairment of a guard, and the availability but non-universality of alternative designs, led the court to uphold the jury's verdict that the design was not defectively unreasonable.
